citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
One adult female rabbit had healing wounds on its nose and face, with moderate scabbing, crusting, and fur loss present
along the midline of the nose. Though the facility stated they hadn’t noticed these findings prior to this inspection, the
lesions observed were consistent with irritation that was present prior to today and should have been noted by the facility.
Failure to observe all rabbits daily can result in deterioration of the animal's health, particularly when lesions go unnoticed,
or its etiology is unknown. Daily observation of all animals shall be done to assess their health and well-being. Timely and
accurate information on problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being noted during daily observations must be
communicated to the attending veterinarian so that appropriate veterinary care can be provided.
The facility corrected during this inspection, and to remain corrected from March 11, 2026, forward.

The facility did not have complete acquisition records for seven rabbits and two goats. In addition, disposition records
were not completed in full for one kangaroo, fifteen chickens, one sheep, and two goats.
Acquisition and disposition records, including offspring born at the facility, are necessary to accurately track and identify
animals being used in regulated activities, as well as aid in the detection of movement of animals upon relocation to a new
site. The licensee shall make, keep, and maintain records of acquisition/disposition of all animals that fully and correctly
disclose the required information, as outlined in this section.
Correct by March 27, 2026.

During this inspection, five rabbits were observed entering and exiting their primary enclosure through its main fencing.
The fenceline had areas of wire that has loosened, causing some sections to curl upward and away from the posting.
While the licensee stated they were aware of the needed repairs and were in the process of addressing them, no repairs
have been initiated by the time of this inspection.
Unsuitable openings present within primary enclosures pose the risk of rabbits getting trapped and/or injured when they
maneuver between gaps in the fence line, and are not being safely contained. Such areas that are not properly
maintained can injure the animals housed, especially if sharp points and edges are present. Primary enclosures shall be
structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the rabbits from injury, to contain them, and to keep predators
out.
Correct by April 3, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee and facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
One adult female rabbit had healing wounds on its nose and face, with moderate scabbing, crusting, and fur loss present 
along the midline of the nose. Though the facility stated they hadn’t noticed these findings prior to this inspection, the 
lesions observed were consistent with irritation that was present prior to today and should have been noted by the facility.  
Failure to observe all rabbits daily can result in deterioration of the animal's health, particularly when lesions go unnoticed, 
or its etiology is unknown. Daily observation of all animals shall be done to assess their health and well-being. Timely and 
accurate information on problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being noted during daily observations must be 
communicated to the attending veterinarian so that appropriate veterinary care can be provided. 
 
The facility corrected during this inspection, and to remain corrected from March 11, 2026, forward. 
2.75(b)(1) 
   
Records: Dealers and exhibitors. 
The facility did not have complete acquisition records for seven rabbits and two goats. In addition, disposition records 
were not completed in full for one kangaroo, fifteen chickens, one sheep, and two goats.  
Acquisition and disposition records, including offspring born at the facility, are necessary to accurately track and identify 
animals being used in regulated activities, as well as aid in the detection of movement of animals upon relocation to a new 
site. The licensee shall make, keep, and maintain records of acquisition/disposition of all animals that fully and correctly 
disclose the required information, as outlined in this section. 
 
Correct by March 27, 2026. 
3.53(a)(1) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
During this inspection, five rabbits were observed entering and exiting their primary enclosure through its main fencing. 
The fenceline had areas of wire that has loosened, causing some sections to curl upward and away from the posting. 
While the licensee stated they were aware of the needed repairs and were in the process of addressing them, no repairs 
have been initiated by the time of this inspection.

Unsuitable openings present within primary enclosures pose the risk of rabbits getting trapped and/or injured when they 
maneuver between gaps in the fence line, and are not being safely contained. Such areas that are not properly 
maintained can injure the animals housed, especially if sharp points and edges are present. Primary enclosures shall be 
structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the rabbits from injury, to contain them, and to keep predators 
out. 
 
Correct by April 3, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee and facility representatives.
